message.content: Adversaries may exploit the Windows Application Compatibility Infrastructure, specifically through application shimming, to establish persistence and elevate privileges. Application shimming allows backward compatibility for software across different Windows versions by acting as a buffer between the program and the OS. Malicious actors can leverage shims to bypass User Account Control (UAC), inject DLLs, disable security features, and intercept memory addresses. This can lead to elevated privileges, installation of backdoors, and disabling of defenses like Windows Defender. Notable groups such as FIN7, Pillowmint, and SDBbot have used application shimming for persistence. Mitigation strategies include monitoring for unusual shim database modifications, restricting administrative privileges, and employing application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized shim installations. Regularly auditing and updating security policies can also help in detecting and preventing such exploitation.
